comments.text: A log-free improvement follows from the results of Conlon--Fox--Pham: https://www.its.caltech.edu/~dconlon/subset_sums.pdf and Szemerédi--Vu: https://annals.math.princeton.edu/wp-content/uploads/annals-v163-n1-p01.pdf. Write $\Sigma(X)$ for the finite subset sums of $X$. Theorem 1.9 from the work of Conlon--Fox--Pham, inserted into the proof of Szemerédi--Vu, Corollary 1.4, gives an absolute $C$ such that, if $X(x)\ge C\sqrt{x}$ eventually, then $\{dm:m\ge M\}\subseteq\Sigma(X)$ for some $d,M\ge1$. Indeed, in Szemerédi--Vu's Lemma 6.4 the common differences are replaced successively by gcds, so the progressions remain homogeneous; their residue-correction and Graham-net argument then produces a tail of $d\mathbb N$. Now suppose that $A$ satisfies the condition of the problem and $\liminf_{x\to\infty}A(x)/\sqrt{x}>C$. Choose an infinite $D=\{u_j\}\subset A$ with $u_j\ge j^4$, and put $X=A\setminus D$. Then $D(x)=O(x^{1/4})$, so $X(x)\ge C\sqrt{x}$ eventually. Hence $\{dm:m\ge M\}\subseteq\Sigma(X)$. By pigeonhole, there are $u<v$ in $D$ with $u\equiv v\pmod d$ and $v-u\ge Md$. Thus $v-u\in\Sigma(X)$, so $v=u+\sum_{s\in S}s$ for some nonempty finite $S\subset X$, a contradiction. Therefore $\liminf_{x\to\infty}A(x)/\sqrt{x}\le C$. AI Acknowledgement: this connection between the recent results of Conlon--Fox--Pham and this problem was made independently by GPT-5.5 without human involvement.
